core_behavior.note: All three models agree on credential-based authentication as the core behavior, but none specifies or questions what 'fast' means, and all assume password-based auth without acknowledging unspecified alternatives like OAuth or SSO.

inputs.note: Model A specifies username with length constraints, Model B allows email or username without length limits, and Model C specifies a max-254-character identifier plus additional fields like client_metadata and remember_me not present in A or B.

outputs.note: Model A returns a JWT or session ID, Model B returns a success/failure message with optional 2FA prompt, and Model C returns a detailed HTTP 200 with JWT access token, opaque refresh token, HTTP status codes, and structured error codes.

assumptions.note: Model A assumes MySQL and Redis caching, Model B assumes secure cookies for session management, and Model C assumes Argon2id hashing with specific parameters and stateless JWT with server-side refresh token storage.

failure_modes.note: Model A specifies account lockout after 5 failed attempts with admin alerts, Model B specifies lockout with user unlock instructions, and Model C specifies structured error codes per failure type without specifying a lockout threshold.

edge_cases.note: Model A covers non-ASCII characters and concurrent logins, Model B covers brute force as an edge case, and Model C focuses on MFA code variants, remember_me behavior, and legacy bcrypt fallback.

security_constraints.note: Model A references OWASP guidelines and IP blocking, Model B adds CSRF and XSS protections and audit logging, and Model C specifies Argon2id parameters, HttpOnly/SameSite cookie attributes, and server-side refresh token revocation.

performance_constraints.note: Model A targets 500ms average response time with 100 concurrent requests/second, Model B targets 200ms with 10,000 concurrent requests, and Model C states only 'defined performance thresholds' without a numeric latency target.

summary: ASM (Assumption Divergence) was the most frequently assigned category, appearing in four of the eight fields, reflecting that the underspecified requirement caused models to independently invent incompatible design choices.
